payload.publisher: New York City Independent Budget Office

tool_args.label: New York City Independent Budget Office (IBO)

payload.entity_name: New York City Independent Budget Office
payload.description: IBO's mission is to enhance understanding of New York City's budget, public policy, and economy through independent analysis.

warnings: entity.parent_entity is relation context, not a closed enum or identity key. Parent entities are relation context. Suggested relation: part_of_agency.

payload.entity_name: New York City Department of Transportation

payload.description: de Blasio Administration's 2019 plan to improve bus service across the City, focused on increasing citywide bus speeds by 25% and reversing downward ridership trends, establishing goals such as improving 5 miles of existing bus lanes each year, installing 10-15 miles of new bus lanes each year, expanding bus lane camera enforcement, and advocating with the MTA for all-door boarding.

payload.project_name: Staten Island Express Bus Network Redesign

payload.description: A bus-only street in Lower Manhattan, from 6 a.m. to 10 p.m., only trucks and buses are allowed to travel through the corridor. Started as a pilot in 2019, later made permanent.

DOT's five-year master transportation plan covering the use of streets, sidewalks, and pedestrian spaces across the City, developed to comply with Local Law 195 of 2019, covering calendar years 2022 through 2026.

Also known as the New York City Streets Plan mandate. Required DOT to construct 150 miles of protected bus lanes in a 5-year period, among other street and transit benchmarks. Codified as NYC Administrative Code § 19-199.1.
MTA's Central Business District Tolling Program, commonly called congestion pricing, commenced in Central and Lower Manhattan.

payload.raw_value_text: over 3,000 buses by the end of 2028

payload.claim_text: New York City buses are among the slowest in the United States.
payload.description: According to 2018 Federal Transit Administration data, MTA Bus Company averaged 8.15 mph and MTA New York City Transit averaged 7.07 mph, ranking at the bottom relative to other large U.S. cities.

payload.claim_text: DOT is not on track to meet the 150-mile protected bus lane mandate of Local Law 195 by 2026.
Despite several initiatives aimed at increasing bus speeds, bus speeds citywide in December 2024 stayed largely consistent with those in January 2019.

payload.description: Bus speeds increased by as much as 24% after the 14th Street busway pilot
Ridership increased by as much as 30% after the 14th Street busway pilot

payload.scope: MTA public bus network, New York City

payload.claim_text: The MTA bus network runs over 300 routes across over 3,200 miles in New York City.
The M15/M15 SBS route carried over 16 million passengers in 2023.

payload.description: Local Law 195 of 2019 directed DOT to develop and implement the NYC Streets Plan, a 5-year transportation master plan.
DOT is the agency responsible for implementing the NYC Streets Plan.
DOT is required to meet benchmarks set by Local Law 195 of 2019, including building 150 miles of protected bus lanes.
NYPD is responsible for enforcing bus lane and traffic rules through issuance of citations, while DOT sets the rules for parking violation codes.
MTA operates the bus network, including M15/M15 SBS routes
MTA Bus Company and New York City Transit are both owned and run by the MTA.
MTA Bus Company and New York City Transit are both owned and run by the MTA.
The MTA presented Bus Network Redesign initiative. Staten Island Express Bus Network Redesign has been completed and implemented.
The MTA presented Bus Network Redesign initiative. Bronx Local Bus Network Redesign has been completed and implemented.
City Council passed Local Law 195 of 2019 in November 2019.
The City started a pilot program that transformed 14th Street into a busway. DOT implemented and later made the pilot permanent.

payload.raw_value_text: grew by $323 million, or about 28%, from $1.2 billion in 2021 to $1.5 billion in Preliminary Budget for FY2025
